title: Q&A: How Capcom Brought Path Tracing to RE ENGINE Across PRAGMATA and Resident Evil Requiem | NVIDIA Technical Blog
gist: Capcom's RE ENGINE team spent two years building a full path tracer (covering both direct and indirect lighting) for simultaneous release in Resident Evil Requiem and PRAGMATA, leaning on NVIDIA RTX Kit and DLSS Ray Reconstruction for denoising while custom-building components like ScreenSpaceAlphaTest and their own ReSTIR GI implementation, and restructuring the entire asset/content pipeline around reflection- and lighting-aware production.

themes.theme: Path tracing as a AAA production milestone

insights.claim: Capcom's RE ENGINE team spent two years building first a reference path tracer validated against common DCC (digital content creation) path tracers, then a game-optimized version scaled for real-time use, before shipping path tracing simultaneously in two titles with different visual identities (Resident Evil Requiem and PRAGMATA).
insights.speaker: Phillip Singh
insights.so_what: A two-year R&D cycle to reach production-grade real-time path tracing sets an expectation for how long other AAA studios need to budget for similar RTX Kit adoption — relevant for gauging pace of path-tracing adoption across the industry.
insights.locator: How did you evolve from a ray tracing pipeline

insights.claim: Unlike prior ray-tracing implementations that used ray tracing only for indirect lighting, both titles now compute direct lighting through the path tracer as well, replacing shadow maps and their resolution limitations, which Capcom says significantly reduced the visual gap between gameplay and cutscenes.
insights.speaker: Phillip Singh
insights.so_what: Indicates the shift from selective ray tracing to full path tracing is now feasible in shipping AAA titles, a proof point for RTX Kit's real-time viability that could accelerate adoption at other studios.
insights.quote: Because path tracing is active at all times, the visual gap between gameplay and cutscenes has been greatly reduced.
insights.locator: How does the path tracing implementation differ

insights.claim: NVIDIA DLSS Ray Reconstruction is described as a critical foundation providing faster and more stable denoising than Capcom's prior hand-written shader-based denoising solutions.
insights.speaker: Phillip Singh
insights.so_what: Confirms DLSS Ray Reconstruction is becoming a load-bearing dependency for real-time path tracing in AAA games, reinforcing NVIDIA's software moat around its RTX ecosystem beyond raw GPU hardware sales.
insights.quote: NVIDIA DLSS Ray Reconstruction provides faster and more stable denoising than hand-written shader-based solutions and serves as a critical foundation for our implementation.
insights.locator: How did you ensure both ray tracing and path tracing

insights.claim: Capcom engineered a custom lightweight ScreenSpaceAlphaTest method — comparing ray-hit position against screen-space depth — instead of performing expensive per-ray-hit texture lookups, to make alpha-cutout geometry (like damaged areas with missing geometry) affordable under path tracing.
insights.speaker: Phillip Singh
insights.so_what: Illustrates that vendor toolkits (RTX Kit) still require significant in-house engineering workarounds for edge cases (alpha geometry) — a reminder that real-time path tracing adoption isn't plug-and-play even with NVIDIA tooling.
insights.locator: How did you ensure both ray tracing and path tracing

insights.claim: For noise reduction in indoor, IBL-dominant scenes, Capcom chose to reimplement ReSTIR GI themselves from the published research paper rather than using the version bundled in NVIDIA RTX Kit.
insights.speaker: Phillip Singh
insights.so_what: A notable data point that a flagship RTX Kit adopter still bypasses NVIDIA's own reference implementation for a core denoising component — suggests RTX Kit's off-the-shelf pieces don't yet fully satisfy top-tier studio requirements.
insights.quote: Rather than using the implementation in RTX Kit, we reimplemented ReSTIR GI ourselves based on the published paper.
insights.locator: When using NVIDIA RTX Kit and DLSS 4

insights.claim: Capcom frames the introduction of path tracing partly as a way to give high-end PC users experiences achievable only on powerful hardware, alongside years of R&D and maturing external technology.
insights.speaker: Phillip Singh
insights.so_what: Signals continued demand-side incentive for high-end GPU upgrades tied to exclusive graphical fidelity — supportive of NVIDIA's premium GPU segment and the broader RTX hardware upgrade cycle narrative.
insights.quote: We also wanted to provide high-end PC users with experiences that can only be achieved on powerful hardware.
insights.locator: Why was now the time for Capcom

insights.claim: Path tracing changed Capcom's content pipeline: for Resident Evil Requiem it forced review of asset production, implementation rules and quality verification (not just lighting), and for PRAGMATA it required reflection-aware asset production because surface precision, normals, and material settings directly affect the final path-traced image.
insights.speaker: Phillip Singh
insights.so_what: Suggests real-time path tracing adoption carries hidden downstream costs in art/production pipelines, not just engine engineering — relevant to estimating true total cost of adoption for studios evaluating RTX Kit.
insights.locator: How did path tracing change the content pipeline

insights.claim: Strand hair rendering (used for both Grace in Resident Evil Requiem and Diana in PRAGMATA) benefited significantly from real-time light transmission enabled by path tracing, contributing to volume and material realism.
insights.speaker: Phillip Singh
insights.so_what: Strand hair plus path tracing is emerging as a visible showcase feature for RTX-class hardware marketing — a recurring selling point NVIDIA and partners can use to differentiate high-end GPU tiers.
insights.locator: How did path tracing change the content pipeline
